en: How does your company ensure that site specific induction, training and instruction are carried out?
e.g. site meetings, toolbox talks, bulletins or other means.
How does your company consult with workers about any changes and progress of the project?
A construction project will be non-notifiable where the work lasts for fewer than 30 days or involves fewer than 500 person days of work.
Does your company act as a Principal Contractor for all non-notifiable projects?

A construction project will be notifiable where the work lasts for more than 30 days and has more than 20 workers on site at any one time, or involves more than 500 person days of work (for example, 50 people working for more than 10 days).
Does your company act as a Principal Contractor for all notifiable projects?

It is a key duty of a Principal Contractor to co-ordinate the construction phase of a project which includes liaising with the Client and Principal Designer, preparing the construction phase plan, and organising contractors and their work.
Describe how your company plans, manages, monitors and co-ordinates the construction phase of a project.
Please provide details of the experience, training, qualifications, and knowledge of health & safety and arrangements of your designers.
Does your company have procedures in place to ensure that your designs meet the requirements of The Workplace (Health, Safety and Welfare) Regulations 1992?

Your aim is to eliminate hazards from the design (so far as is reasonably practicable), and reduce risks from any remaining hazards – giving priority to collective protective measures before individual protective measures. The consideration of hazard and risk is integrated within the design process, so there is no need to carry out a separate ’design risk assessment’. A design review may be useful as a means of checking that the principal aim of eliminating hazards or reducing risks is achieved. A design practice or design team may choose to develop their own arrangements to make sure that designers are aware of their responsibility to eliminate hazards and reduce risks. The nature of these arrangements will depend on the size of the design practice/team and the type of work undertaken. In general, the production of design risk assessments that cover well-known and understood construction hazards should be avoided. The crucial information, ie that which covers unusual or complex hazards, is then much less likely to be missed and should be passed on to those that will need it in a format that is readily accessible to them.
Does your company ensure hazards are eliminated and risks are reduced in the design process?
Does your company co-ordinate and communicate with the client, other designers, the Principal Designer and Principal Contractor and contractors?

When you employ or control people doing work for you, you must ensure that they have the necessary skills, knowledge, training and experience to do the job safely and without putting their own or others' health and safety at risk.
